# Part 2. Full Text

# Section I. Funding Opportunity Description

# Statutory Authority

This program is authorized under Section 317K of the Public Health Service Act, \[42 U.S.C.\
\
247b-12\], as amended, and Section 317 of the Public Health Service Act, 42 U.S.C. 247b.

# 1\. Background and Purpose

# Background

The Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS) was initiated in 1987 due to stagnant infant mortality rates \[1\]. PRAMS data are used to examine the associations between risk factors and outcomes, explore disparities by subpopulations, and compare health indicators across jurisdictions. The CDC PRAMS Program provides population-based and jurisdictionspecific estimates on a variety of maternal and infant health indicators that may not be available from any other data source. For example, PRAMS data have been used to monitor progress over time for safe infant sleep position, unintended births, and patterns of health insurance coverage. PRAMS data are used to conduct research at the state and federal level.

PRAMS provides jurisdiction-specific and population-based data on women recently delivering a live birth or stillborn infant. Because less than $5 %$ of the general population is pregnant at any time \[2\], there is a need for data that purposely samples from this population to provide stable estimates that can be stratified by population subgroup, as well as to provide jurisdictionspecific estimates of maternal experiences and behaviors that occur before, during, and shortly after pregnancy.

The data collection processes for PRAMS has been fine-tuned over time to achieve timely, high quality scientific data that meets the needs of various stakeholders by maximizing response rates, while minimizing respondent burden \[3\]. The CDC PRAMS Program continually assesses PRAMS methods and procedures through comparisons with industry standards, consultation with organizations funded to implement PRAMS, other experts in the field, and feedback from the PRAMS data collectors. A new version of the questionnaire, with potential adaptations to the existing PRAMS methodology (e.g., online data collection) will occur under this NOFO. Questions included on the PRAMS questionnaire are cognitively tested to ensure respondents understand survey questions as intended. The core PRAMS surveillance methodology, including study design, data collection procedures, questionnaires and sampling, is documented in the PRAMS protocol \[1, 3, 4\].

The CDC PRAMS Program regularly collaborates with other federal agencies to identify other sources of maternal and infant data that may be duplicative and identify opportunities to leverage and learn from other data collection systems. In recent years, PRAMS has also proven to be a versatile system to address emerging health issues through the use of questionnaire supplements \[3\]. Leveraging the PRAMS infrastructure has allowed survey supplements on emerging topics to be quickly implemented across impacted funded sites to provide timely data for analysis and dissemination. To date, survey supplements have been developed and implemented for a variety of topics, including the H1N1 flu pandemic, family history of breast and ovarian cancer, e-cigarette use, Zika virus, marijuana and prescription opioid drug use, maternal disability, and disaster preparedness. The PRAMS methodology has been adapted to conduct surveillance among women with a recent stillbirth, and to conduct a telephone call-back survey at 9 months postpartum in jurisdictions with high rates of opioid-related overdose deaths \[5\]. The PRAMS surveillance system has also been leveraged to inform efforts of the Health Resources and Services Administration’s (HRSA) Maternal and Child Health Bureau by oversampling Healthy Start clients to allow comparisons with similar populations not participating in Healthy Start \[6\].

# Purpose

The purpose of PRAMS is to collect vital records jurisdiction-specific and population-based data on maternal and child health indicators (MCH) of high scientific quality that can be used to monitor the prevalence of maternal behaviors and experiences to inform programs and systems changes that influence maternal and infant health, as well as conduct research.

The intent of this NOFO is to collect PRAMS data that can be used by federal agencies, states, jurisdictions, clinical groups, policymakers, and researchers to monitor the prevalence of maternal behaviors and experiences and to assess progress on maternal and infant health indicators (e.g., Healthy People 2030 targets, Health Resources and Services Administration’s (HRSA) Title V Maternal and Child Health Program performance measures) \[7-9\]; to investigate emerging issues in maternal and infant health; to support research; and to assess impacts of programs and system changes aimed at improving the health of mothers and infants.

Researchers have used PRAMS data to answer research and programmatic questions of interest to the field of maternal and child health using analytical techniques, such as econometric and epidemiological modeling. Findings from PRAMS data collection are also used to inform programs and policies that improve maternal and child health as described in CDC PRAMS Data to Action Success Stories \[10\] collected from exisiting and previous awardees. Results are translated into useable information for public health action, including fact sheets, presentations, and educational materials. The CDC PRAMS Program releases reports on surveillance topics and key indicators and highlight new publications on the CDC PRAMS website ( [https://www.cdc.gov/prams/](https://www.cdc.gov/prams/)) \[1\].

# Healthy People 2020 and other National Strategic Priorities

PRAMS data will be used to monitor progress towards four HP2030 objectives related to Maternal, Infant, and Child Health (3 of which are developmental) \[9\]:

1. Increase the proportion of infants who are put to sleep on their backs.
2. (Developmental) Increase the proportion of infants who are put to sleep in a safe sleep environment.
3. (Developmental) Increase the percentage of women who are screened for postpartum depression at their postpartum checkup.
4. (Developmental) Decrease the proportion of pregnant women who use illicit opioid pain relievers during pregnancy.

# Public Health Impact

PRAMS surveillance data will be used to establish an evidence base for data-driven public health decision making and program development. Data will be used to: 1) document estimates and trends in maternal and infant indicators; 2) identify subpopulations at greatest risk for the adverse birth outcomes and inform prevention strategies; 3) inform the HRSA Title V needs assessment process to help jurisdictions plan for and allocate MCH block grant funds; 4) develop programs and system changes that support improvement of maternal and infant health; 5) investigate emerging issues in maternal and infant health; and 6) support health professionals to incorporate the latest research findings into their standards of practice.

# Relevant Work

Historically, the PRAMS surveillance project has been a collaboration between the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and health departments, which were funded to collect jurisdiction-specific, population-based data on maternal behaviors and experiences before, during, and shortly after pregnancy to provide information on a wide range of risk factors that influence maternal and infant health.

PRAMS has collected information from women with a recent live birth since inception of the project, but there has been a data gap for women who have a pregnancy that results in a loss, such as a stillbirth, defined as a fetal death at 20 weeks of gestation or more. In 2016, CDC funded one site to adapt PRAMS methodology and data collection tools to assess maternal behaviors and experiences before, during and shortly after pregnancy among women with a recent stillbirth \[5, 11\]. Essential data for monitoring stillbirth and related factors such as periconceptional, antenatal, and postpartum health care after a pregnancy loss were obtained.

PRAMS data has been used in the assessment of CDC Winnable Battles ( [https://www.cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov/) /winnablebattles/) \[12\] as they relate to maternal and infant health:

 Opioids

 HIV elimination

 Vaccine Preventable Disease

Other ongoing CDC activities in the National Center for Chronic Disease Prevention and Health Promotion’s Division of Reproductive Health that complement this activity include the Maternal and Child Health Epidemiology Program (MCHEP), Enhancing Reviews and Surveillance to Eliminate Maternal Mortality Program (ERASE MM), Perinatal Quality Collaboratives (PQCs), CDC’s Opioid Response Coordination Unit activities that encompass Rapid Assessment of Maternal Opioid Use and Overdose to Improve Outcomes and Save Lives. PRAMS data support these activities by providing population-based information on jurisdictionlevel MCH priorities to help inform the initiatives and programs of the MCHEP, PQCs, and Maternal Mortality Review Committees (MMRCs) \[13-16\].

# 2\. Approach

The Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System collects jurisdiction- and population-based information on selected maternal behaviors and experiences that occur prior to, during, and shortly after pregnancy. Awardees will implement major project components, including representative sampling, data collection operations, project staffing, convening of a steering committee, data analysis, and data translation according to the PRAMS protocol. The expected outcomes are to produce high quality data representative of the awardee’s population that can be used to monitor prevalence of maternal behaviors and experiences to inform programs, research, and system changes that influence maternal and infant health.

This announcement includes 3 separate funding components:

Component A: Core Surveillance Component B: Point-in-time Tribal Surveillance Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance

Applicants may apply for Component A or B, but not both. To be funded for Component $C ,$ , applicants must also apply for and receive funding for Component A. Separate applications are required for each Component.

# Component A: Core Surveillance 5-year Period of Performance

Component A is to conduct standardized ongoing core PRAMS surveillance, using a core survey with approximately 55-questions, among women with a recent live birth. Sites

conducting PRAMS surveillance may adapt methods and implement survey supplements to respond to emerging issues that arise during the data collection cycle including response to postdisaster or emergency surveillance needs.

Requirements for Component A are listed below:

# Data Collection

1. Collaborate with the Office of Vital Records, the official office to record all births within the jurisdiction to ensure availability of data for monthly sampling, data quality checks, an accurate sampling frame, and final year birth files.
2. Develop and implement a sampling stratification plan that addresses data collection among subpopulations that are disproportionately affected by adverse maternal and infant health outcomes (e.g., based on race, ethnicity, or geography).
3. Adhere to data collection procedures as documented in the current CDC PRAMS protocol or any future updates of the protocol, including closing and submitting of each monthly batch of data to CDC within the 95-day data collection period and maintaining telephone operations with maximum recommended calls to each sampled woman throughout the data collection process. All monthly batches for the entire year should be submitted to CDC by June 30 of the year following the data collection year.
4. Achieve the minimum annual response rate set by CDC, which is currently $5 5 %$
5. Use data collection software provided by CDC for PRAMS operations.
6. Maintain adequate staffing to conduct data collection activities and project operations, including a suggested minimum of 2 full-time equivalents, between CDC funded and inkind support, committed to working on daily operations and project coordination, and a PD/PI dedicating at least $10 %$ time to the project to support the PD/PI responsibilities outlined in Section VI.4. Cooperative Agreement Terms and Conditions of Award.
7. Submit final year birth files to CDC by September 1 of the year following the data collection year (i.e., 9/1/22 for 2021 data collection); the birth file must include all the elements required by CDC for data weighting.
8. Provide input into the development of a new version of the PRAMS questionnaire, and implement the new questionnaire within the specified timeline with potential adaptations to the existing PRAMS methodology (e.g., online data collection).

# Data Analysis and Research

1. Form a Steering Committee as outlined in the PRAMS protocol to advise the jurisdiction’s PRAMS project in the development and selection of questions for the survey and on the use, dissemination, and application of findings; steering committees are expected to meet annually beginning during the first year post-award.
2. Develop an analysis plan by the 8th month post award to be updated annually at the end of each calendar year that reflects the jurisdiction’s priorities for PRAMS analyses.
3. Analyze and disseminate jurisdiction-specific PRAMS results annually, within 1 year of receiving a weighted dataset from CDC.
4. Sign a data sharing agreement that will be provided by CDC, to allow CDC to release a pre-determined, restricted set of birth certificate variables to share with internal and external stakeholders and researchers for analytic purposes, per terms of the data sharing agreement between CDC and awardees for the duration of the period of performance.

CDC will provide one standard PRAMS Data Sharing Agreement at the time of award as opposed to separate agreements for resource purposes and to facilitate data sharing on behalf of awardees. Compliance with this data sharing agreement is required to ensure that the objectives of the NOFO are met.

# Data Translation

1. Collaborate with the maternal and child health (MCH) unit of the applicant jurisdiction’s approved Title V recipient to identify contact information for sampled women and to assist with the selection of questions for the survey and on the use, dissemination, and application of findings.

2. Document two data-to-action examples using the CDC-provided template annually and submit to CDC with the yearly continuation application.


# Capacity Building

1. Participate in routine conference calls to discuss operational and analytic activities with CDC; participate in webinars and conference calls organized by CDC to discuss issues that affect all awardees; and attend in-person awardee meetings hosted by CDC.

# Component B: Point-in-Time Surveillance Tribal Surveillance 3-year period of performance

Component B is to conduct point-in-time (one time, one birth year) PRAMS surveillance on selected maternal behaviors and experiences that occur prior to, during, and shortly after pregnancy among women with a recent live birth for federally-recognized American Indian Tribes, Alaska Native Villages, or Urban Indian Organizations (UIOs) with at least 1,000 live births annually or tribal organizations that support American Indian Tribes, Alaska Native Villages or Urban Indian Organizations. To provide reliable statistical estimates, the proposed project should cover an area with at least 1,000 live births annually to American Indian/Alaska Native women.

Requirements for Component B are listed below:

# Data Collection

1. Collaborate with the Office of Vital Records, the official office to record all births within the jurisdiction to ensure availability of data for monthly sampling, data quality checks, and final year birth files.
2. Develop and implement a sampling stratification plan that addresses data collection among subpopulations that are disproportionately affected by adverse maternal and infant health outcomes (e.g., based on race, ethnicity, or geography).
3. Adhere to data collection procedures as documented in the CDC PRAMS protocol, including closing and submitting of each monthly batch of data to CDC within the 95- day data collection period and maintaining telephone operations with maximum recommended calls to each sampled woman throughout the data collection process. All monthly batches for the entire year should be submitted to CDC by June 30 of the year

following the data collection year.

4. Achieve the minimum annual response rate set by CDC, which is currently $5 5 %$

5. Use data collection software provided by CDC for PRAMS operations.

6. Maintain adequate staffing to conduct data collection activities and project operations, including a suggested minimum of 2 full-time equivalents, between CDC funded and inkind support, committed to working on daily operations and project coordination, and a PD/PI dedicating at least $10 %$ time to the project to support the PD/PI responsibilities outlined in Section VI.4. Cooperative Agreement Terms and Conditions of Award.

7. Submit final year birth files to CDC by September 1 of the year following the data collection year (i.e., 9/1/22 for 2021 data collection); the birth file must include all the elements required by CDC for data weighting.


# Data Analysis and Research

1. Form a Steering Committee, as outlined in the PRAMS protocol, to advise the awardee’s PRAMS project in the development and selection of questions for the survey and on the use, dissemination, and application of findings; steering committees are expected to meet annually beginning during the first year post-award.

2. Develop an analysis plan by the 8th month post award that reflects the jurisdiction’s priorities for PRAMS analyses.

3. Analyze and disseminate jurisdiction-specific PRAMS results beginning within 1 year of receiving a weighted dataset from CDC.

4. Sign a data sharing agreement that will be provided by CDC, to allow CDC to release a pre-determined, restricted set of birth certificate variables to share with internal and external stakeholders and researchers for analytic purposes, per terms of the data sharing agreement between CDC and awardees for the duration of the period of performance.


CDC will provide one standard PRAMS Data Sharing Agreement at the time of award as opposed to separate agreements for resource purposes and to facilitate data sharing on behalf of awardees. Compliance with this data sharing agreement is required to ensure that the objectives of the NOFO are met.

# Data Translation

1. Collaborate with the maternal and child health (MCH) unit of the applicant jurisdiction’s approved Title V recipient to identify contact information for sampled women and to assist with the use, dissemination, and application of findings.

2. Document two data-to-action examples using the CDC-provided template within 1 year of receiving a weighted dataset from CDC.


# Capacity Building

1. Participate in routine conference calls to discuss operational and analytic activities with CDC; participate in webinars and conference calls organized by CDC to discuss issues that affect all awardees; and attend in-person awardee meetings hosted by CDC.

# Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance

# 3-year period of performance

Component C is to conduct standardized PRAMS surveillance among women who recently experienced a stillbirth. PRAMS stillbirth surveillance is a stand-alone project with a separate survey (approximately 60 questions) that will be used to gather data for monitoring stillbirth occurrence and related factors such as periconceptional, antenatal, and postpartum health care after a stillbirth. The information collected will fill data gaps and inform efforts to help prevent the occurrence of stillbirth and improve care and provide support to women who experience stillbirth.

Requirements for Component C are listed below:

# Data Collection

1. Collaborate with the Office of Vital Records, the official office to record all fetal deaths within the jurisdiction to ensure availability of data for monthly sampling, data quality checks, and final year fetal death files.
2. Develop and implement a sampling stratification plan that addresses data collection among subpopulations that are disproportionately affected by stillbirth (e.g., based on race, ethnicity, or geography).
3. Adhere to data collection procedures as documented in the CDC PRAMS Stillbirth protocol, including closing and submitting of each monthly batch of data to CDC within the 95-day data collection period and maintaining telephone operations with maximum recommended calls to each sampled woman throughout the data collection process. All monthly batches for the entire year should be submitted to CDC by June 30 of the year following the data collection year.
4. Achieve the minimum annual response rate set by CDC, which is currently $5 5 %$ .
5. Use data collection software provided by CDC for PRAMS operations.
6. Maintain adequate staffing to conduct data collection activities and project operations, including a suggested minimum of 2 full-time equivalents, between CDC funded and inkind support, committed to working on daily operations and project coordination, and a PD/PI dedicating at least $10 %$ time across components A and C to support the PD/PI responsibilities outlined in Section VI.4. Cooperative Agreement Terms and Conditions of Award.
7. Submit final year fetal death files to CDC by December 1 of the year following the data collection year (i.e.,12/1/22 for 2021 data collection); the fetal death file must include all the elements required by CDC for data weighting.

# Data Analysis and Research

1. Form a Steering Committee, as outlined in the PRAMS protocol, to advise the jurisdiction’s PRAMS project in the development and selection of questions for the survey and on the use, dissemination, and application of findings.

2. Develop an analysis plan by the 8th month post award to be updated annually at the end of each calendar year that reflects the jurisdiction’s priorities for PRAMS analyses; steering committees are expected to meet annually beginning during the first year postaward.

3. Analyze and disseminate jurisdiction-specific PRAMS results within 1 year of receiving


a weighted dataset from CDC.

4. Sign a data sharing agreement that will be provided by CDC, to allow CDC to release a pre-determined, restricted set of birth certificate variables to share with internal and external stakeholders and researchers for analytic purposes, per terms of the data sharing agreement between CDC and awardees for the duration of the period of performance. CDC will provide one standard PRAMS Data Sharing Agreement at the time of award as opposed to separate agreements for resource purposes and to facilitate data sharing on behalf of awardees. Compliance with this data sharing agreement is required to ensure that the objectives of the NOFO are met.

# Data Translation

1. Collaborate with the maternal and child health (MCH) unit of the applicant jurisdiction’s approved Title V recipient to identify contact information for sampled women and to assist with the selection of questions for the survey and on the use, dissemination, and application of findings.

2. Document two data-to-action examples using the CDC-provided template within 1 year of receiving a weighted dataset from CDC.


# Capacity Building

1. Participate in routine conference calls to discuss operational and analytic activities with CDC; participate in webinars and conference calls organized by CDC to discuss issues that affect all awardees; and attend in-person awardee meetings hosted by CDC.

# Objectives/Outcomes

# Component A: Core Surveillance

Objectives:

1. To implement population-based surveillance within a defined vital records jurisdiction on selected maternal behaviors and experiences that occur prior to, during, and shortly after pregnancy among women with a recent live birth, including emerging issues and post-disaster surveillance needs, as they arise.

2. To ensure data is of high scientific quality and comparable to other jurisdictions by following the methodology documented in the CDC PRAMS protocol.

3. To conduct comprehensive analyses of PRAMS data based on a written jurisdictionspecific analysis plan designed to inform programmatic activities, research, and public health practice.

4. To translate and disseminate analytic results into useable information for public health action that can guide program development and evaluation, in collaboration with a jurisdiction-level steering committee.


# Expected outcomes:

1. Produce timely, high quality data representative of the jurisdiction’s population to monitor risk factors for maternal and infant health.

2. Analyze and disseminate PRAMS data on a variety of MCH topics to inform public health programs, research and system changes, and health care system changes.

3. Document efforts to translate data into useable information for public health action.

4. Make PRAMS data available to stakeholders including public health agencies, clinical providers, health systems, and researchers for studies to inform knowledge gaps in maternal and infant health.


# Component B: Point-in-Time Surveillance Tribal Surveillance

Objectives:

1. To implement point-in-time (one time, one birth year) PRAMS surveillance on selected maternal behaviors and experiences that occur prior to, during, and shortly after pregnancy among women with a recent live birth for federally-recognized American Indian Tribes, Alaska Native Villages, or Urban Indian Organizations (UIOs) with at least 1,000 live births annually or tribal organizations that support American Indian Tribes, Alaska Native Villages or Urban Indian Organizations with a project area that covers at least 1,000 live births annually.
2. To ensure data is of high scientific quality and comparable to other jurisdictions by following the methodology documented in the CDC PRAMS protocol.
3. To conduct comprehensive analyses of PRAMS data based on a written project-specific analysis plan designed to inform programmatic activities and public health practice.
4. To translate and disseminate analytic results into useable information for public health action that can guide program development and evaluation, in collaboration with a project-level steering committee.

# Expected outcomes:

1. Produce timely, high quality data representative of awardee’s population to monitor risk factors for maternal and infant health.
2. Analyze and disseminate PRAMS data on a variety of MCH topics to inform public health programs, research and system changes, and health care system changes.
3. Document efforts to translate data into useable information for public health action.
4. Make PRAMS data available to stakeholders, including tribes, tribal organizations, public health agencies, clinical providers, health systems, and researchers for studies to inform knowledge gaps in maternal and infant health.

# Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance

# Objectives:

1. To implement population-based surveillance within a defined vital records jurisdiction on selected maternal behaviors and experiences that occur prior to, during, and shortly after pregnancy among women who recently experienced a stillbirth.

2. To ensure data is of high scientific quality by using the standardized CDC PRAMS


stillbirth protocol.

3. To conduct comprehensive analyses of PRAMS stillbirth data based on a written jurisdiction-specific analysis plan designed to inform programmatic activities, research, and public health practice related to stillbirth.
4. To translate and disseminate analytic results into useable information for public health action that can guide program development and evaluation in collaboration with a jurisdiction-level steering committee.

# Expected outcomes:

1. Produce timely, high quality data representative of the vital records jurisdiction population, to monitor risk factors for stillbirth occurrence.
2. Analyze and disseminate PRAMS data on a variety of MCH topics to inform prevention efforts and clinical practice to prevent stillbirths.
3. Document efforts to translate stillbirth data into useable information for public health action.
4. Make PRAMS data available to stakeholders including federal, state, and local public health agencies, clinical providers, health systems, and researchers for studies to inform knowledge gaps in stillbirth research.

# Target Population

Component A: Core Surveillance - This NOFO component addresses the population of women with a recent live birth in a defined vital records jurisdiction. This group will benefit directly from implementation of programs, research, and system changes aimed at improving the health of mothers and infants jurisdiction-wide.

Components B: Point-in-Time Tribal Surveillance - This NOFO component addresses the population of American Indian or Alaska Native women with a recent live birth in a defined area with at least 1,000 live births to American Indian or Alaska Native women annually. This group will benefit directly from implementation of programs, research, and system changes aimed at improving the health of American Indian and Alaska Native mothers and infants.

Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance - This NOFO component addresses the population of women experiencing a recent stillbirth in a defined vital records jurisdiction. This group will benefit directly from implementation of programs, research, and system changes aimed at reducing fetal deaths at 20 weeks of gestation or more.

# Collaboration/Partnerships

# Component A: Core Surveillance

Awardees are required to collaborate with the Office of Vital Records, the official office to record all births within the jurisdiction. This collaboration is critical to conducting PRAMS. Implementation of the activities under this funding announcement requires ongoing timely access to vital records information for monthly sampling, as well as timely availability of final year birth files for data weighting. The final vital statistics birth files are needed for weighting the annual data set. The final birth file is to be available to CDC by September 1 of the year following data collection and must include all the elements required by CDC for data weighting.

Awardees are required to collaborate with the maternal and child health (MCH) unit of the jurisdiction’s approved Title V recipient. This is necessary since PRAMS data will be used to monitor jurisdiction-specific and national performance objectives for the Title V Maternal and Child Health (MCH) Program, a federal block grant which serves as a key source of support for promoting and improving the health and well-being of the nation’s mothers, children, including children with special needs, and their families. The PRAMS data will serve to benchmark select Title V performance measures and enable jurisdictions to demonstrate the impacts of Title V on selected health outcomes within the jurisdiction. This collaboration is critical to ensuring that the PRAMS project collects the data that are needed to support the jurisdiction’s MCH Title V grant reporting requirements, emerging issues, and other jurisdictional priorities in MCH. In addition, implementation of PRAMS activities requires access to jurisdiction-level maternal and child health data sources, many of which are funded, maintained, or accessible through partnership with the jurisdiction’s Title V recipient. These data sources, such as high-risk infant screening, newborn metabolic screening, Sudden Unexpected Infant Death (SUID), and birth defects programs, Medicaid, the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) \[17\], or others are needed in order to locate participant contact information for the target population.

Awardees are required to form a Steering Committee to advise the jurisdiction’s PRAMS project on the development and selection of questions for the survey and on the use, dissemination, and application of findings. The committee should include a multi-disciplinary mixture of individuals from the public and private sector, as well as from the academic community. Steering Committees should include a representative from the Vital Registrars, jurisdiction-level health department maternal and child health unit. Other members may include stakeholders in maternal and infant health such as family planning, substance abuse prevention, oral health, behavioral health, SUID, epidemiology units, state Medicaid programs, state Special Supplemental Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) programs, hospital associations, state chapters of professional medical organizations, perinatal quality collaboratives, regional/local health departments, tribes or tribal organizations, foundations, and public health departments of state and/or local universities.

Awardees whose vital records jurisdiction includes tribal populations that are served by a Tribal Epidemiology Center (TEC) are encouraged, but not required, to work with the TEC on surveillance of their American Indian/Alaska Native (AI/AN) populations. The activities of TECs consist of surveillance to develop and evaluate MCH programmatic activities in coordination with tribes and urban AI/AN communities. In addition, they serve as liaisons with counties, states, the Indian Health Service (IHS), and other Federal public health authorities, in order to improve public health capacity for surveillance and research within AI/AN communities.

Awardees are expected to leverage funding or other in-kind resources to maximize project outcomes. Recipients should consider collaborating with relevant state and local agencies, community-based organizations, and national, non-governmental organizations external to CDC that have similar interests in the health and well-being of mothers and infants.

Awardees may volunteer to be part of the PRAMS Coordinating Committee. The PRAMS Coordinating Committee (CC) will be comprised of CDC Project Scientists and awardees. The PRAMS Coordinating Committee will help with project planning and to promote exchange of information and best practices for program development and evaluation.

# Evaluation/Performance Measurement

The awardees are expected to create an evaluation plan by the 8th month post award that assesses fidelity to data collection protocols, examines key data collection operations indicators using reports generated from the CDC-provided software system, monitors data quality, assesses overall and subpopulation response rates, provides timely access to data for processing and weighting, analyzes data, and demonstrates use of these data in informing system changes, research, and programs. Evaluation plans will be refined in collaboration with CDC post-award.

# Translation Plan

# Components A, B and C

Awardees are expected to develop a research translation and dissemination plan by the $8 ^ { \\mathrm { t h } }$ month post award, in collaboration with steering committee members to ensure surveillance findings are translated into system changes or programs that address maternal and infant health. Translation plans will be refined in collaboration with CDC post-award. The plans should:

1. Describe the data priority topics for PRAMS analyses.

2. Describe partnerships, existing or proposed, for dissemination and translation and how findings will be disseminated through various channels, including steering committee members, health policy makers, and health providers.

3. Describe how data will be translated into public health action.

4. Track and document examples of data to action translation.


# Data Management Plan (DMP)

Awardees will implement a standard CDC PRAMS Data Management Plan (DMP). CDC PRAMS will provide the DMP following award.

OMB/PRA will apply.

# Special Eligibility

For an application to be considered, the application must be responsive to these Special Eligibility Requirements. If your application is incomplete or non-responsive to the special eligibility requirements listed in this section, it will not enter into the review process.

The special eligibility requirements address the key components of PRAMS that are integral to establishing and maintaining successful standardized surveillance of women with a recent live birth or stillbirth.

# Component A: Core Surveillance

1. Throughout the 5-year period of performance, applicants must have:

a. timely (i.e. between two and four months after delivery) access to edited birth certification information from the Vital Records Office in the vital records jurisdiction for sampling on a monthly basis; and b. timely access to the final birth file for submission to CDC by September 1 of the year following the data collection year (i.e., 9/1/22 for 2021 data collection), and the file must include all the elements required by CDC for data weighting.

2. Applicants must be able to release a pre-determined, restricted set of birth certificate variables to CDC to share with internal and external stakeholders and researchers for analytic purposes, per terms of the data sharing agreement between CDC and awardees. Note: CDC will provide the PRAMS Data Sharing Agreement at the time of award. Compliance with this data sharing agreement is required. The current data sharing agreement is located in the PRAMS protocol Appendix R, “Data Sharing.”

B. Collaboration with Maternal and Child Health Unit

1. Applicants must have support from the maternal and child health (MCH) unit of the applicant vital records jurisdiction’s approved Title V recipient ( [https://mchb.tvisdata.hrsa.gov/](https://mchb.tvisdata.hrsa.gov/)) to facilitate access to jurisdiction-level maternal and child health data sources throughout the 5-year period of performance.

C. CDC Access to PRAMS Questionnaire and Operational Data

1. Applicants must be able to provide CDC with unrestricted access to PRAMS operational and questionnaire data throughout the 5-year period of performance.

2. Applicant organizations must complete the following registrations as described in the SF 424 (R&R) Application Guide to be eligible to apply for or receive an award. All applicant organizations must register with Grants.gov. Please visit [www.Grants.gov](http://www.grants.gov/) at least 30 days prior to submitting your application to familiarize yourself with the registration and submission processes. The “one-time” registration process will take three to five days to complete. However, it is best to start the registration process at least two weeks prior to application submission.

All Program Directors/Principal Investigators (PD/PIs) must also work with their institutional officials to register with the eRA Commons or ensure their existing Principle Investigator (PD/PI) eRA Commons account is affiliated with the eRA commons account of the applicant organization. All registrations must be successfully completed and active before the application due date. Applicant organizations are strongly encouraged to start the eRA Commons registration process at least four (4) weeks prior to the application due date. ASSIST requires that applicant users have active eRA Commons account in order to prepare an application. It also requires that the applicant organization's Signing Official have an active eRA Commons Signing Official account in order to initiate the submission process. During the submission process, ASSIST will prompt the Signing Official to enter their Grants.gov Authorized Organizational Representative (AOR) credentials in order to complete the submission, therefore the applicant organization must ensure that their Grants.gov AOR credentials are active.

# 7\. Universal Identifier Requirements and System for Award Management (SAM)

All applicant organizations must obtain a DUN and Bradstreet (D&B) Data Universal Numbering System (DUNS) number as the Universal Identifier when applying for Federal grants or cooperative agreements. The DUNS number is a nine-digit number assigned by Dun and Bradstreet Information Services. An AOR should be consulted to determine the appropriate number. If the organization does not have a DUNS number, an AOR should complete the US D&B D-U-N-S Number Request Web Form or contact Dun and Bradstreet by telephone directly at 1-866-705-5711 (toll-free) to obtain one. A DUNS number will be provided immediately by telephone at no charge. Note this is an organizational number. Individual Program Directors/Principal Investigators do not need to register for a DUNS number.

Additionally, all applicant organizations must register in the System for Award Management (SAM). Organizations must maintain the registration with current information at all times during which it has an application under consideration for funding by CDC and, if an award is made, until a final financial report is submitted or the final payment is received, whichever is later. SAM is the primary registrant database for the Federal government and is the repository into which an entity must provide information required for the conduct of business as a recipient. Additional information about registration procedures may be found at the SAM internet site at [https://www.sam.gov/index.html](https://www.sam.gov/index.html).

If an award is granted, the recipient organization must notify potential sub-recipients that no organization may receive a subaward under the grant unless the organization has provided its DUNS number to the recipient organization.

( [https://www.hhs.gov/sites/default/files/grants/grants/policies-regulations/hhsgps107.pdf](https://www.hhs.gov/sites/default/files/grants/grants/policies-regulations/hhsgps107.pdf)), applications received in response to the same Notice of Funding Opportunity generally are scored individually and then ranked with other applications under peer review in their order of relative programmatic, technical, or scientific merit. Applicants that plan to collect public health data must submit a Data Management Plan (DMP) in the Resource Sharing Plan section of the PHS 398 Research Plan Component of the

application. A DMP is required for each collection of public health data proposed. Applicants who contend that the public health data they collect or create are not appropriate for release must justify that contention in the DMP submitted with their application for CDC funds. The DMP may be outlined in a narrative format or as a checklist but, at a minimum, should include: • A description of the data to be collected or generated in the proposed project;

• Standards to be used for the collected or generated data;

• Mechanisms for, or limitations to, providing access to and sharing of the data (include a description of

provisions for the protection of privacy, confidentiality, security, intellectual property, or other rights -

this section should address access to identifiable and de-identified data);

• Statement of the use of data standards that ensure all released data have appropriate

documentation that

describes the method of collection, what the data represent, and potential limitations for use; and

• Plans for archiving and long-term preservation of the data, or explaining why long-term preservation

and access are not justified (this section should address archiving and preservation of identifiable and deidentified

data).

Examples of DMPs may be found here: USGS, [http://www.usgs.gov/products/data-andtools/data-management/data-management-plans](http://www.usgs.gov/products/data-andtools/data-management/data-management-plans)

The standard CDC PRAMS

DMP will be provided to awardees post award.

# RESEARCH PLAN

 Applicants should clearly indicate the Component(s) for which they are applying and should include at the beginning of the title, the respective Component under which the application should be considered (i.e. Component A: Core Surveillance, Component B: Point-in-Time Tribal Surveillance, or Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance).

 Applicants may apply for Component A: Core Surveillance or Component B: Point-inTime Tribal Surveillance, not both.

 To be funded for Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance, applicants must also apply for and receive Component A: Core Surveillance funding.

 A separate application must be submitted for each Component.

The applicant’s research plan should address activities that will be conducted over the entire period of performance and should include the following items:

# Component A: Core Surveillance

5-year period of performance

Background and Need

1. Describe the vital records jurisdiction. Describe the composition of the birth population, giving the numbers of overall live births and live births in each sub-population of interest within the vital records jurisdiction.
2. Describe how subpopulations in the jurisdiction that are disproportionately affected by adverse maternal and infant health outcomes (e.g., based on race, ethnicity, or geography) and strategies for using PRAMS data to address these disparities, such as implementing stratified sampling based on various characteristics to make inferences about subgroups of high public health interest. This may be assessed in relation to relevant national rates, jurisdiction program activities, HRSA Maternal and Child Health Bureau indicators, or the Healthy People 2030 Objectives.
3. Describe how the proposed research will improve or have an impact on population health.
4. Describe the maternal and child health priorities for the jurisdiction and how PRAMS data can be integrated into the jurisdiction’s activities to address those priorities and evaluate jurisdiction programs.
5. Identify gaps in needed information concerning risk factors for adverse pregnancy and

infant outcomes and describe how PRAMS data can be used to fill these gaps and aid in program development.

6. Describe how data from PRAMS will complement the jurisdiction’s use of vital records or other MCH data by increasing understanding of maternal and infant health issues, and evaluating programs designed to address them.

Profile of Jurisdiction Birth Registration and Sampling Process

1. Describe plans for obtaining monthly birth samples including:

A. Describe the jurisdiction’s process for registering births and drawing monthly samples. Include each step from collection of information at the birth site, having an initial computerized file, and having a clean, edited file from which other information can be drawn.

B. Present a timeline for the cleaning of critical variables, such as name, address, and date of birth.

C. Provide a timeline or flow chart documenting that the birth certificate information is available within two to four months after the date of birth for sampling.

D. Describe the schedule on which birth certificate information will be available for monthly sampling.

2\. Describe any jurisdiction laws or policies that place restrictions on the release of vital records data to outside organizations for surveillance purposes and indicate the impact of these laws or policies on core PRAMS operations.

3\. Describe any jurisdiction laws or policies that place restrictions on the temporary secure upload of information to the CDC-provided software system for use by jurisdiction-level PRAMS staff to contact sampled women, and describe a plan for addressing any restrictions.

4\. Describe plans for identifying a Vital Statistics representative to liaise with the PRAMS program to develop and maintain the sampling program and make any modifications needed throughout the life of the program.

5\. Describe plans for ensuring any changes in the vital statistics system, such as with file layouts or adoption of any new birth certificate format, will be communicated in writing to the awardee PRAMS program in a timely fashion.

# Management and Staffing Plan

1. Describe the proposed management and staffing plan for the study. Include a jurisdiction-level staffing plan (roles, percentage of time on project) that identifies and describes the jurisdiction staff, including those who will contribute to PRAMS throughout the period of performance. 2. Describe plans for maintaining Project Activities when staff vacancies occur. 3. Document (with curriculum vitae) and describe experience and expertise of proposed staff, including ability to conduct the proposed study. Describe the proposed team’s experience collecting surveillance data, including, but is not limited to surveillance and surveys, epidemiology and research, statistical methods employed when weighted survey data are used, translation of study findings, collaboration and partnership.

2. Document (with curriculum vitae) and describe the experience of project leadership overseeing or conducting PRAMS, PRAMS-like, or other surveillance activities within the jurisdiction, and level of performance in achieving project aims. Include project and agency organizational charts with key personnel.

3. Describe plans for ensuring the program maintains local IRB approval and new key personnel or other significant contributors involved in the design or conduct of research involving human subjects meet educational program requirements in the protection of human subjects.


# Analysis and Translation plan

1. Document the extent to which PRAMS, PRAMS-like or other surveillance data have been analyzed and used for health program planning, program evaluation, policy development, and resource allocation.
2. Provide a list of abstracts, presentations, publications (e.g. fact sheets, surveillance reports, journal articles, etc.), and specific examples of dissemination and translation of PRAMS, PRAMS-like or other surveillance data from within the last 3 years.
3. Describe any jurisdiction laws or policies that place restrictions on the re-release of birth certificate variables to internal and external stakeholders or researchers for analytic purposes, and describe a plan for addressing them. If none, please indicate.
4. Describe the specific roles and responsibilities of staff responsible for identifying analytic priorities, developing analysis plans, and analyzing PRAMS data.
5. Describe a plan for data use that integrates the MCH priorities that can be addressed by PRAMS, addresses disparities, and will be used for program planning, program evaluation, policy development, and resource allocation.

In accordance with the United States Protecting Life in Global Health Assistance policy, all non-governmental organization (NGO) applicants acknowledge that foreign NGOs that receive funds provided through this award, either as a prime recipient or subrecipient, are strictly prohibited, regardless of the source of funds, from performing abortions as a method of family planning or engaging in any activity that promotes abortion as a method of family planning, or to provide financial support to any other foreign non-governmental organization that conducts such activities. See Additional Requirement (AR) 35 for applicability

( [https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-35.html](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-35.html)).

For more information on expanded authority and pre-award costs, go

to: [https://www.hhs.gov/sites/default/files/grants/grants/policies-regulations/hhsgps107.pdf](https://www.hhs.gov/sites/default/files/grants/grants/policies-regulations/hhsgps107.pdf). CDC requires that mechanisms for, and cost of, public health data sharing be included in grants, cooperative agreements, and contracts. The cost of sharing or archiving public health data may also be included as part of the total budget requested for first-time or continuation awards. Fulfilling the data-sharing requirement must be documented in a Data Management Plan (DMP) that is developed during the project planning phase prior to the initiation of generating or collecting public health data and must be included in the Resource Sharing Plan(s) section of the PHS398 Research Plan Component of the application.

Applicants who contend that the public health data they collect or create are not appropriate for release must justify that contention in the DMP submitted with their application for CDC funds (for example, privacy and confidentiality considerations, embargo issues).

Recipients who fail to release public health data in a timely fashion will be subject to

procedures normally used to address lack of compliance (for example, reduction in funding, restriction of funds, or award termination) consistent with 45 CFR 74.62 or other authorities as appropriate. For further information, please

see: [https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-25.html](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-25.html) for revised AR-25.

# Risk Assessment Questionnaire Requirement

CDC is required to conduct pre-award risk assessments to determine the risk an applicant poses to meeting federal programmatic and administrative requirements by taking into account issues such as financial instability, insufficient management systems, non-compliance with award conditions, the charging of unallowable costs, and inexperience. The risk assessment will include an evaluation of the applicant’s CDC Risk Questionnaire, located at [https://www.cdc.gov/grants/documents/PPMR-G-CDC-Risk-Questionnaire.pdf](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/documents/PPMR-G-CDC-Risk-Questionnaire.pdf), as well as a review of the applicant’s history in all available systems; including OMB-designated repositories of government-wide eligibility and financial integrity systems (see 45 CFR 75.205(a)), and other sources of historical information. These systems include, but are not limited to: FAPIIS ( [https://www.fapiis.gov/](https://www.fapiis.gov/)), including past performance on federal contracts as per Duncan Hunter National Defense Authorization Act of 2009; Do Not Pay list; and System for Award Management (SAM) exclusions.

CDC requires all applicants to complete the Risk Questionnaire, OMB Control Number 0920- 1132 annually. This questionnaire, which is located at

[https://www.cdc.gov/grants/documents/PPMR-G-CDC-Risk-Questionnaire.pdf](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/documents/PPMR-G-CDC-Risk-Questionnaire.pdf), along with supporting documentation must be submitted with your application by the closing date of the Notice of Funding Opportunity Announcement. Upload the questionnaire and supporting documents as an attachment in the "12. Other Attachments" section of the "RESEARCH & RELATED Other Project Information" section of the application. If your organization has completed CDC’s Risk Questionnaire within the past 12 months of the closing date of this NOFO, then you must submit a copy of that questionnaire, or submit a letter signed by the authorized organization representative to include the original submission date, organization’s EIN and DUNS.

When uploading supporting documentation for the Risk Questionnaire into this application package, clearly label the documents for easy identification of the type of documentation. For example, a copy of Procurement policy submitted in response to the questionnaire may be labeled using the following format: Risk Questionnaire Supporting Documents \_ Procurement Policy.

# Duplication of Efforts

Applicants are responsible for reporting if this application will result in programmatic, budgetary, or commitment overlap with another application or award (i.e. grant, cooperative agreement, or contract) submitted to another funding source in the same fiscal year. Programmatic overlap occurs when (1) substantially the same project is proposed in more than one application or is submitted to two or more funding sources for review and funding

consideration or (2) a specific objective and the project design for accomplishing the objective are the same or closely related in two or more applications or awards, regardless of the funding source. Budgetary overlap occurs when duplicate or equivalent budgetary items (e.g., equipment, salaries) are requested in an application but already are provided by another source. Commitment overlap occurs when an individual’s time commitment exceeds 100 percent, whether or not salary support is requested in the application. Overlap, whether programmatic, budgetary, or commitment of an individual’s effort greater than 100 percent, is not permitted. Any overlap will be resolved by the CDC with the applicant and the PD/PI prior to award. Report Submission: The applicant must upload the report under “Other Attachment Forms.” The document should be labeled: "Report on Programmatic, Budgetary, and Commitment Overlap.”

# Section V. Application Review Information

# 1\. Criteria

Only the review criteria described below will be considered in the review process. As part of the CDC mission (http:// [www.cdc.gov/](http://www.cdc.gov/) about/ organization/ mission.htm), all applications submitted to the CDC in support of public health research are evaluated for scientific and technical merit through the CDC peer review system.

# Overall Impact

Reviewers will provide an overall impact/priority score to reflect their assessment of the likelihood for the project to exert a sustained, powerful influence on the research field(s) involved, in consideration of the following review criteria and additional review criteria (as applicable for the project proposed).

# Scored Review Criteria

Reviewers will consider each of the review criteria below in the determination of scientific merit, and give a separate score for each. An application does not need to be strong in all categories to be judged likely to have major scientific impact. For example, a project that by its nature is not innovative may be essential to advance a field.

# Significance

Does the project address an important problem or a critical barrier to progress in the field? If the aims of the project are achieved, how will scientific knowledge, technical capability, and/or clinical practice be improved? How will successful completion of the aims change the concepts, methods, technologies, treatments, services, or preventative interventions that drive this field?

# Component A: Core Surveillance and Component B: Point-in-Time Tribal Surveillance

 To what extent does the applicant justify the need for the Core Surveillance or Point-intime surveillance of tribal populations based on the burden of adverse pregnancy and infant outcomes?

 To what extent do disparities exist, within sub-populations, or defined geographical areas?

 How do outcomes compare to relevant national rates, the HRSA Maternal and Child

Health Bureau indicators, and the Healthy People 2030 Objectives?

 To what extent does the applicant describe the surveillance information needed and how it may be used for health program planning, program evaluation, policy development, and resource allocation?

 To what extent does the applicant describe how the research will improve or impact population health, or address disparities?

 To what extent does the applicant describe how new information from PRAMS will compliment vital records data and address the jurisdiction's or project's maternal and child health priorities?

# Component C: Stillbirth Surveillance

 To what extent does the applicant justify the need for this program?  Does the applicant adequately describe the population of women with stillbirths? Is the applicant’s rationale for conducting the enhanced activities appropriate?  To what extent does the applicant describe how the stillbirth surveillance activities may be used to improve health outcomes and services for women with recent stillbirths?

# Investigator(s)

Are the PD/PIs, collaborators, and other researchers well suited to the project? Have they demonstrated an ongoing record of accomplishments that have advanced their field(s)? If the project is collaborative or multi-PD/PI, do the investigators have complementary and integrated expertise; are their leadership approach, governance and organizational structure appropriate for the project?

# Component A: Core Surveillance and Component B: Point-in-Time Tribal Surveillance

 Does the applicant adequately document the experience of project leadership and their ability to achieve project aims related to PRAMS or other surveillance activities?

To what extent do the roles and responsibilities for organizational units supporting PRAMS appear reasonable and appropriate, particularly for coordination with the jurisdiction’s vital records agency, as well as other relevant partners such as state or jurisdiction-level MCH and other organizational units?

 To what extent are the roles and responsibilities for key personnel and their expertise and experience documented and appear reasonable and appropriate? Does their placement within the organization appear to be appropriate?

 Is the PD/PI time on to the project sufficient to support the PD/PI responsibility outlined in Section VI.4. Cooperative Agreement Terms and Conditions of Award?

 To what extent does the applicant provide a reasonable and appropriate staffing plan including a description of staff contributions, roles, time to PRAMS surveillance activities?

 Does the applicant adequately describe the composition and function of the steering committee?

 Does the applicant adequately document the experience of project leadership and their ability to achieve project aims related to PRAMS or other surveillance activities?

As applicable for the project proposed, reviewers will evaluate the following additional items while determining scientific and technical merit, and in providing an overall impact/priority score, but will not give separate scores for these items.

# Protections for Human Subjects

If the research involves human subjects but does not involve one of the six categories of research that are exempt under 45 CFR Part 46, the committee will evaluate the justification for involvement of human subjects and the proposed protections from research risk relating to their participation according to the following five review criteria: 1) risk to subjects, 2) adequacy of protection against risks, 3) potential benefits to the subjects and others, 4) importance of the knowledge to be gained, and 5) data and safety monitoring for clinical trials.

For research that involves human subjects and meets the criteria for one or more of the six categories of research that are exempt under 45 CFR Part 46, the committee will evaluate: 1) the justification for the exemption, 2) human subjects involvement and characteristics, and 3) sources of materials. For additional information on review of the Human Subjects section, please refer to the HHS/CDC Requirements under AR-1 Human Subjects Requirements ( [https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-1.html](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-1.html)).

If your proposed research involves the use of human data and/or biological specimens, you must provide a justification for your claim that no human subjects are involved in the Protection of Human Subjects section of the Research Plan.

# Inclusion of Women, Minorities, and Children

When the proposed project involves clinical research, the committee will evaluate the proposed plans for inclusion of minorities and members of both genders, as well as the inclusion of children. For additional information on review of the Inclusion section, please refer to the policy on the Inclusion of Women and Racial and Ethnic Minorities in Research ( [https://www.cdc.gov/maso/Policy/Policy\_women.pdf](https://www.cdc.gov/maso/Policy/Policy_women.pdf) and the policy on the Inclusion of Persons Under 21 in Research ( [https://www.cdc.gov/maso/Policy/policy496.pdf](https://www.cdc.gov/maso/Policy/policy496.pdf)).

# Vertebrate Animals

The committee will evaluate the involvement of live vertebrate animals as part of the scientific assessment according to the following four points: 1) proposed use of the animals, and species, strains, ages, sex, and numbers to be used; 2) justifications for the use of animals and for the appropriateness of the species and numbers proposed; 3) procedures for limiting discomfort, distress, pain and injury to that which is unavoidable in the conduct of scientifically sound research including the use of analgesic, anesthetic, and tranquilizing drugs and/or comfortable restraining devices; and 4) methods of euthanasia and reason for selection if not consistent with the AVMA Guidelines on Euthanasia. For additional information on review of the Vertebrate Animals section, please refer to the Worksheet for Review of the Vertebrate Animal Section ( [https://grants.nih.gov/grants/olaw/VASchecklist.pdf](https://grants.nih.gov/grants/olaw/VASchecklist.pdf)).

# Biohazards

Reviewers will assess whether materials or procedures proposed are potentially hazardous to research personnel and/or the environment, and if needed, determine whether adequate protection is proposed.

# Dual Use Research of Concern

Reviewers will identify whether the project involves one of the agents or toxins described in the US Government Policy for the Institutional Oversight of Life Sciences Dual Use Research of Concern, and, if so, whether the applicant has identified an IRE to assess the project for DURC potential and develop mitigation strategies if needed.

For more information about this Policy and other policies regarding dual use research of concern, visit the U.S. Government Science, Safety, Security (S3) website at: [http://www.phe.gov/s3/dualuse](http://www.phe.gov/s3/dualuse). Tools and guidance for assessing DURC potential may be found at: [http://www.phe.gov/s3/dualuse/Pages/companion-guide.aspx](http://www.phe.gov/s3/dualuse/Pages/companion-guide.aspx).

# 3\. Additional Review Considerations

As applicable for the project proposed, reviewers will consider each of the following items, but will not give scores for these items, and should not consider them in providing an overall impact/priority score.

Applicants are not required to submit a Data Management Plan (DMP) in the Resource Sharing Plan section of the PHS 398 Research Plan Component of the application. Awardees will implement a standard CDC PRAMS Data Management Plan (DMP) for AR-25 [https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-25.html](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-25.html). The standard CDC PRAMS DMP will be provided to awardees post award.

# Resource Sharing Plan(s)

HHS/CDC policy requires that recipients of grant awards make research resources and data readily available for research purposes to qualified individuals within the scientific community after publication. Please see: [https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-25.html](https://www.cdc.gov/grants/additionalrequirements/ar-25.html)

New additional requirement: CDC requires recipients for projects and programs that involve data collection or generation of data with federal funds to develop and submit a Data Management Plan (DMP) for each collection of public health data.

Investigators responding to this Notice of Funding Opportunity should include a detailed DMP in the Resource Sharing Plan(s) section of the PHS 398 Research Plan Component of the application. The AR-25 outlines the components of a DMP and provides additional information for investigators regarding the requirements for data accessibility, storage, and preservation.

The DMP should be developed during the project planning phase prior to the initiation of collecting or generating public health data and will be submitted with the application. The submitted DMP will be evaluated for completeness and quality at the time of submission.

The DMP should include, at a minimum, a description of the following:

• A description of the data to be collected or generated in the proposed project;

• Standards to be used for the collected or generated data;

• Mechanisms for, or limitations to, providing access to and sharing of the data (include a description of provisions for the protection of privacy, confidentiality, security, intellectual property, or other rights - this section should address access to identifiable and de-identified data);

• Statement of the use of data standards that ensure all released data have appropriate documentation that describes the method of collection, what the data represent, and potential limitations for use; and

• Plans for archiving and long-term preservation of the data, or explaining why long-term preservation and access are not justified (this section should address archiving and

preservation

of identifiable and de-identified data).

Applications submitted without the required DMP may be deemed ineligible for award unless submission of DMP is deferred to a later period depending on the type of award, in which case, funding restrictions may be imposed pending submission and evaluation.

# Budget and Period of Support

Reviewers will consider whether the budget and the requested period of support are fully justified and reasonable in relation to the proposed research. The applicant can obtain guidance for completing a detailed justified budget on the CDC website, at the following Internet address: [http://www.cdc.gov/grants/interestedinapplying/applicationresources.html](http://www.cdc.gov/grants/interestedinapplying/applicationresources.html)

The budget can include both direct costs and indirect costs as allowed.

Indirect costs could include the cost of collecting, managing, sharing and preserving data. Indirect costs on grants awarded to foreign organizations and foreign public entities and performed fully outside of the territorial limits of the U.S. may be paid to support the costs of compliance with federal requirements at a fixed rate of eight percent of modified total direct costs exclusive of tuition and related fees, direct expenditures for equipment, and subawards in excess of $$ 25,000$ . Negotiated indirect costs may be paid to the American University, Beirut, and the World Health Organization.

Indirect costs on training grants are limited to a fixed rate of eight percent of MTDC exclusive of tuition and related fees, direct expenditures for equipment, and sub-awards in excess of $$ 25,000$ .

If requesting indirect costs in the budget based on a federally negotiated rate, a copy of the indirect cost rate agreement is required. Include a copy of the current negotiated federal indirect cost rate agreement or cost allocation plan approval letter.

# 4\. Review and Selection Process

Applications will be evaluated for scientific and technical merit by an appropriate peer review group, in accordance with CDC peer review policy and procedures, using the stated review criteria.

As part of the scientific peer review, all applications:

 Will undergo a selection process in which all responsive applications will be discussed and assigned an overall impact/priority score.

 Will receive a written critique.

Applications will be assigned to the appropriate HHS/CDC Center, Institute, or Office. Applications will compete for available funds with all other recommended applications submitted in response to this NOFO. Following initial peer review, recommended applications will receive a second level of review. The following will be considered in making funding recommendations:

 Scientific and technical merit of the proposed project as determined by scientific peer review.

 Availability of funds.

 Relevance of the proposed project to program priorities.

One organization per vital records jurisdiction will be funded for Components A and C.

Additionally for Component A, the following will be considered:

 Size of birth population annually  Racial or ethnic diversity of surveillance population

Additionally for Components C, the following will be considered:

 Number of annual fetal deaths  Racial or ethnic diversity of surveillance population

# Review of risk posed by applicants.

Prior to making a Federal award, CDC is required by 31 U.S.C. 3321 and 41 U.S.C. 2313 to review information available through any OMB-designated repositories of government-wide eligibility qualification or financial integrity information as appropriate. See also suspension and debarment requirements at 2 CFR parts 180 and 376.

In accordance 41 U.S.C. 2313, CDC is required to review the non-public segment of the OMBdesignated integrity and performance system accessible through SAM (currently the Federal Recipient Performance and Integrity Information System (FAPIIS)) prior to making a Federal award where the Federal share is expected to exceed the simplified acquisition threshold, defined in 41 U.S.C. 134, over the period of performance. At a minimum, the information in the system for a prior Federal award recipient must demonstrate a satisfactory record of executing programs or activities under Federal grants, cooperative agreements, or

procurement awards; and integrity and business ethics. CDC may make a Federal award to a recipient who does not fully meet these standards, if it is determined that the information is not relevant to the current Federal award under consideration or there are specific conditions that can appropriately mitigate the effects of the non-Federal entity's risk in accordance with 45 CFR $\ S 7 5 . 2 0 7$ .

CDC’s framework for evaluating the risks posed by an applicant may incorporate results of the evaluation of the applicant's eligibility or the quality of its application. If it is determined that a Federal award will be made, special conditions that correspond to the degree of risk assessed may be applied to the Federal award. The evaluation criteria is described in this Notice of Funding Opportunity.

In evaluating risks posed by applicants, CDC will use a risk-based approach and may consider any items such as the following:

(1) Financial stability;

(2) Quality of management systems and ability to meet the management standards prescribed in this part;

(3) History of performance. The applicant's record in managing Federal awards, if it is a prior recipient of Federal awards, including timeliness of compliance with applicable reporting requirements, conformance to the terms and conditions of previous Federal awards, and if applicable, the extent to which any previously awarded amounts will be expended prior to future awards;

(4) Reports and findings from audits performed under subpart F 45 CFR 75 or the reports and findings of any other available audits; and

(5) The applicant's ability to effectively implement statutory, regulatory, or other requirements imposed on non-Federal entities.

CDC must comply with the guidelines on government-wide suspension and debarment in 2 CFR part 180, and require non-Federal entities to comply with these provisions. These provisions restrict Federal awards, subawards and contracts with certain parties that are debarred, suspended or otherwise excluded from or ineligible for participation in Federal programs or activities.
